entities.description: The 1532 collected Chaucer print conventionally cited by Skeat as Thynne's edition. It is the sole surviving authority for The Testament of Love and a major printed authority for many selected pieces.
entities.label: William Thynne's 1532 Works of Geoffrey Chaucer

entities.description: The later Thynne-associated Chaucer edition used for comparison in Skeat's Sayings Printed by Caxton. Skeat's errata corrects a heading that mistakenly assigns the Moral Balade to 1542 rather than 1532.
entities.label: 1542 edition of the Works of Geoffrey Chaucer

entities.description: Printed authority used by Skeat for Beware of Doubleness, the warning ballade, and the Balade in Praise of Chaucer.
entities.label: John Stow's 1561 Works of Geoffrey Chaucer

entities.description: The sole surviving textual authority used by Skeat for The Flower and the Leaf and a comparison source for Jack Upland.
entities.label: Thomas Speght's 1598 Works of Geoffrey Chaucer

access.format: Catalog identification and, if available, digital reproduction

entities.description: The early printed copy from which Skeat says his Jack Upland text was written out. Its exact copy-level bibliographic identity remains to be reconciled with the college catalog.

holding.notes: Skeat identifies the holding institution but not a modern shelfmark in the source statement inspected.

entities.label: Early printed copy of Jack Upland at Gonville and Caius College

entities.description: The early print from which Skeat transcribed three short proverbial verse units on p. 450; Caxton does not explicitly attribute the appended sayings to Chaucer.
entities.label: William Caxton's print of Anelida and Arcite with appended sayings

entities.description: An early printed authority collated by Skeat for A Moral Balade; the exact exemplar remains an open bibliographic question.
entities.label: Caxton print containing Scogan's Moral Balade

entities.description: An early Scottish print consulted by Skeat in editing The Complaint of the Black Knight.
entities.label: Chepman and Myllar print of The Complaint of the Black Knight (1508)

entities.description: An early printed authority consulted by Skeat for The Complaint of the Black Knight.

entities.description: John Gower presentation manuscript, circa 1397–1400; The Praise of Peace occupies ff. 5r–10v and was collated by Skeat.
entities.label: British Library, Add MS 59495 (Trentham manuscript)

entities.description: A major fifteenth-century English literary miscellany used extensively by Skeat. Relevant passages are Complaint ff. 20v–30r, Cuckoo ff. 35v–39v, Letter ff. 40r–47r, La Belle Dame ff. 50v–62v, Envoy ff. 147v–148r, and Beware ff. 199r–199bis v.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Fairfax 16

entities.description: Fifteenth-century literary manuscript used for the Complaint, Cuckoo, and Letter. The Complaint copy at ff. 1r–4v is defective because its opening, through line 467, is lost.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Bodl. 638

entities.description: Fifteenth-century literary manuscript used for the Letter, Complaint, Cuckoo, and Envoy. The relevant passages occupy ff. 41r–59r and 97r–101v.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Tanner 346

access.format: Repository-supplied digital images, subject to availability and terms

entities.description: Manuscript authority for Scogan's Moral Balade (ff. 25r–28r), the Commendation of Our Lady (ff. 39v–41r), and Beware of Doubleness (ff. 47v–49r). No complete public digital facsimile was found.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Ashmole 59

entities.description: A major late-medieval Scottish literary manuscript. Relevant passages are Prosperity f. 119r, Complaint ff. 120v–129v, Loyalty f. 138r, Cuckoo ff. 138v–141v, and Letter ff. 211v–217r. The Complaint lacks lines 113–126 and the Cuckoo survives only through line 246.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Arch. Selden B.24

access.accessMethod: Request a complete physical-object facsimile because the public object exposes only three selected images.
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library, MS. Digby 181

entities.description: Manuscript authority for the Letter at ff. 1r–6v and Complaint at ff. 31r–39r. Digital Bodleian currently exposes only three selected images, including f. 2r and two images of f. 39r.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Digby 181

entities.description: A collaborative fifteenth- and early-sixteenth-century household manuscript. Relevant old-foliation passages are Cuckoo ff. 22r–28r, Letter ff. 71r–76v, La Belle Dame ff. 117r–134v, and Good Counsel ff. 147r–150r. Its modern CUDL foliation is respectively 15r–21r, 59r–64v, 98r–115v, and 125r–128r; missing old ff. 77–80 remove the Letter's final twenty stanzas.
entities.label: Cambridge University Library, MS Ff.1.6 (Findern manuscript)

lineage.summary: CME00032 is a U-M electronic selection of twenty-one numbered pieces from Walter W. Skeat's 1897 Chaucerian and Other Pieces, not an encoding of one medieval work and not a complete encoding of the printed volume. It preserves the title page and printed pp. 1–145, 191–203, 205–232, 237–296, 299–326, 347–404, and 449–450. Skeat edited these heterogeneous, historically Chaucer-attributed pieces from early prints and numerous manuscripts while explicitly rejecting Chaucer's authorship. This record distinguishes the repository XML, U-M encoding, scholarly edition, its textual authorities, and later facsimiles. Complete public provider objects are cached where practical, with exact selected-work portions recorded inside each larger object and provider image terms kept separate from public-domain medieval and 1897 texts.

catalogingSubject.scope_note: The U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection from Walter W. Skeat's 1897 Chaucerian and Other Pieces: the printed title page and twenty-one body pieces on pp. 1-145, 191-203, 205-232, 237-296, 299-326, 347-404, and 449-450. It is neither one medieval work nor the complete 1897 volume, and the selected pieces are not works by Geoffrey Chaucer as a group.

contentParts.label: I. The Testament of Love
contentParts.summary: A three-book prose dialogue and allegory of love, attributed to Thomas Usk and dated about 1387; Skeat reconstructed its disordered third book from Thynne's 1532 print.

contentParts.label: III. Jack Upland
contentParts.summary: An anonymous anticlerical prose interrogation, with a short embedded verse passage, edited from an early printed copy at Gonville and Caius College and compared with Speght.

contentParts.label: IV. The Praise of Peace
notes: The XML encodes 404 L elements because headings, Latin matter, or lineation differ from Skeat's stated 385-line work count.
contentParts.summary: John Gower's 385-line appeal to Henry IV for peace and good rule, composed in 1399-1400 and collated against the Trentham manuscript.

contentParts.label: V. The Letter of Cupid
contentParts.summary: Thomas Hoccleve's 476-line 1402 English adaptation of Christine de Pizan's Epistre au dieu d'Amours, edited primarily from Fairfax 16 with extensive manuscript collation.

contentParts.label: VII. A Moral Balade
notes: The XML heading says Thynne 1542, but Skeat's printed errata directs the reader to substitute 1532.
contentParts.summary: Henry Scogan's 189-line counsel poem, edited from early prints and collated with Ashmole 59 and Harley 2251.

contentParts.label: VIII. The Complaint of the Black Knight
contentParts.summary: John Lydgate's 681-line dream-vision complaint, also called A Complaynte of a Lovers Lyfe; its composition date remains debated.

contentParts.label: IX. The Flour of Curtesye
contentParts.summary: A 270-line Valentine poem by John Lydgate, printed by Skeat from Thynne 1532.

contentParts.label: X. A Balade in Commendation of Our Lady
contentParts.summary: John Lydgate's 140-line Marian ballade, restored from Thynne, Ashmole 59, and the complete copy on the terminal vellum leaves of Sloane 1212.

contentParts.label: XI. To My Soverain Lady
contentParts.summary: A 112-line secular Valentine poem by John Lydgate that earlier printed editions joined to the preceding Marian ballade.

contentParts.label: XII. Ballad of Good Counsel
contentParts.summary: John Lydgate's 133-line counsel poem with the refrain 'A wikked tonge wol alway deme amis', collated with Findern and Harley 2251.

contentParts.label: XIII. Beware of Doubleness
contentParts.summary: A 104-line poem by John Lydgate, based by Skeat on Fairfax 16 and compared with Ashmole 59, Harley 7578, Add MS 16165, and Stow 1561.

contentParts.label: XIV. A Balade Warning Men to Beware of Deceitful Women
contentParts.summary: A 49-line antifeminist ballade attributed by Skeat to Lydgate, based on Trinity R.3.19 and corrected from the more complete Trinity O.9.38 copy.

contentParts.label: XVI. La Belle Dame sans Mercy
contentParts.summary: Sir Richard Roos's 856-line English translation of Alain Chartier's 1424 French poem, probably made circa 1441-1450.

contentParts.label: XVIII. The Cuckoo and the Nightingale; or, The Book of Cupid, God of Love
contentParts.summary: A 290-line bird debate now most often attributed to Sir John Clanvowe and probably composed after circa 1386 but before his death in 1391.

contentParts.label: XIX. Envoy to Alison
contentParts.summary: A 27-line anonymous lyric transmitted after the Cuckoo poem but rejected by Skeat as Clanvowe's work.

contentParts.label: XX. The Flower and the Leaf
notes: Skeat counts 595 lines; the XML contains 594 L elements. Speght 1598 is the sole surviving textual authority used by Skeat.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous late-fifteenth-century allegorical dream vision, probably circa 1460-1480; the narrator is a woman, but the author's gender is not established.

contentParts.label: XXI. The Assembly of Ladies
contentParts.summary: An anonymous 756-line late-fifteenth-century dream vision, probably circa 1470-1480; common authorship with The Flower and the Leaf and female authorship both remain debated.

contentParts.label: XXVI. Prosperity
contentParts.summary: An eight-line stanza from the 1410 prologue to John Walton's translation of Boethius, copied in later Scottish form and misattributed to Chaucer in Arch. Selden B.24.

contentParts.label: XXVII. Leaulte Vault Richesse
notes: The XML's nine L elements comprise eight verse lines plus the concluding motto.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous short Scots-form moral poem whose final motto means 'Loyalty is worth riches'; Skeat tentatively places an English original in the later fifteenth century.

contentParts.label: XXVIII. Sayings Printed by Caxton
contentParts.summary: Three anonymous proverbial verse units totaling fourteen lines, printed with Caxton's Anelida and later reprinted in the 1542 Chaucer edition without an attribution by Caxton to Chaucer.

contentParts.label: XXIX. Balade in Praise of Chaucer
contentParts.summary: An anonymous seven-line praise of Chaucer preserved at the end of the Parliament of Fowls in Trinity R.3.19 and printed by Stow in 1561.

extent.description: Twenty-one top-level body pieces. The Testament of Love and Jack Upland are prose-centered but contain eight and four encoded verse lines respectively; the other nineteen pieces are verse. Counts exclude the three verse lines on the title page and include the motto line at the end of Loyalty.

assertion.notes: No manuscript survives; the attribution rests principally on Skeat and Bradley's reconstruction of the acrostic and biographical fit.

notes: The poem is an English adaptation and translation of Christine de Pizan's Epistre au dieu d'Amours.

assertion.notes: The six poems have differing kinds of attribution evidence; the collective statement does not erase those local differences.
Skeat argues for Lydgate on stylistic grounds; the manuscript witnesses used do not uniformly name him.
Harley MS 372 explicitly attributes the English translation to Roos.
Findern ends the poem 'Explicit Clanvowe'. Current scholarship strongly prefers Sir John Clanvowe, while Sir Thomas Clanvowe and a scribal attribution remain minority alternatives.
The stanza is lines 83-90 of the prologue to Walton's translation of Boethius rather than an independent Chaucer poem.
Anonymous status does not imply common authorship. Female authorship of The Flower and the Leaf and The Assembly of Ladies remains debated, and common authorship is not established.

summaries.value: A discontiguous digital selection from Walter W. Skeat's 1897 supplementary Chaucer volume. It combines Thomas Usk's prose Testament of Love and the anonymous polemic Jack Upland with works by Gower, Hoccleve, Scogan, Lydgate, Roos, Walton, probably Sir John Clanvowe, and several anonymous late-medieval writers. The collection documents how non-Chaucerian works entered and were later removed from the Chaucer canon.

CME00032 preserves the 1897 title page and seven printed-page runs: pp. 1-145, 191-203, 205-232, 237-296, 299-326, 347-404, and 449-450. It omits the volume's introduction and preliminaries, pieces II, VI, XV, XVII, XXII-XXV, the notes on pp. 451-554, glossary, and indices.

assertion.notes: The title belongs to Skeat's edition and to the edition-level selection, not to a single medieval composition.
The phrase 'supplement to the Complete Works' describes the publication context and does not assign the selected pieces to Chaucer.
